schools.summary: The apostolic eyewitness to the incarnate Word — 'that which was from the beginning' (the eternal, pre-existent Word, as the Gospel's prologue, John 1:1); the accumulation of verbs 'heard... seen with our eyes... hands have handled' (fullest sensory certainty, not hearsay); 'the Word of life' joining the titles Word and Life; against any who would make Christ a phantom.
Augustine: that hands could handle the Word means the Word was made flesh and dwelt in us — the incarnation the ground of the whole testimony.

themes: that which was from the beginning

schools.summary: The manifestation of eternal life (a parenthesis explaining 'the Word of life') — 'the life was manifested' (ephanerothe, making visible what already existed eternal and unseen; the Life is Christ, the fountain of life); 'we have seen it, and bear witness' (sight grounding witness, witness issuing in preaching); 'that eternal life, which was with the Father' (the Life dwelling from eternity in communion with the Father, the language of personal pre-existence), now made known to men.

The goal of the testimony — 'that which we have seen and heard' (the third assertion, hammering home certainty, nothing by hearsay); 'declare we unto you, that ye also may have fellowship with us' (the gospel gathering believers into shared partnership); rising to its height 'our fellowship is with the Father, and with his Son Jesus Christ' (communion not merely human but divine — to be joined to the apostles is to be brought into fellowship with God himself); fellowship, not mere information, the goal.

themes: with the Father and the Son

schools.summary: The aim of fullness of joy — echoing Christ's words to the disciples (John 15:11; 16:24), John taking up his Master's language; 'that your joy may be full' (pepleromene, perfect, the settled state of brimming completeness, not partial or fleeting); flowing from the fellowship just described — communion with the Father and Son the spring of the deepest happiness (Calvin: the perfect blessedness through the gospel); the joy of faith (Wesley); John writing to gladden, not burden.

themes: that your joy may be full

schools.summary: The central message from Christ — 'God is light' (a comprehensive figure: God's wisdom, holiness, purity, glory, and love; what light is to the natural eye, God is to the spiritual, Wesley); 'in him is no darkness at all' (no admixture of ignorance, sin, error, or evil, no contrary principle); the foundation for the moral argument — because God is light, fellowship with him demands walking in light; 'light' speaking at once of holiness and self-revealing glory.

The first false profession tested — 'if we say that we have fellowship with him' (professing communion with God) 'and walk in darkness' (yet living in sin, the life flatly contradicting the claim); the verdict 'we lie, and do not the truth' (false in word and deed, neither speaking nor practising truth); Calvin's logic of inconsistency — since God is light and sanctifies his own, claiming fellowship while in darkness is self-contradiction; striking at religion of mere profession divorced from holiness.

themes: if we say we have fellowship

schools.summary: The true reality — 'if we walk in the light, as he is in the light' (a life of holiness, truth, and openness, conformed to the God who is light, 1 Tim 6:16); the first fruit 'we have fellowship one with another' (communion with God and among believers, the thing the false professor only claimed); the second 'the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin' (present tense, ongoing purification even as we walk in light); light and atonement joined, guarding against both the lawless and the perfectionist.

themes: if we walk in the light
the blood cleanseth from all sin

schools.summary: The denial of sin condemned — 'if we say that we have no sin' (professing no inward principle or taint of sin, to be already perfect; a real error, perhaps the Gnostics who held matter evil but the soul untainted and so denied guilt, Robertson); 'we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us' (self-deceived, the saving truth absent); commending grace from its necessity — since no one is free from sin, all need pardon, and to deny sin spurns the grace that saves (Calvin); guarding the cleansing blood of v7.

themes: if we say we have no sin
the truth is not in us

schools.summary: The promise to confession — 'if we confess our sins' (homologomen, present, continual honest acknowledgment to God, not hiding or excusing); the ground in God's character 'he is faithful and just' — faithful because he promised, and (strikingly) just, not merely merciful; God's very justice securing pardon because Christ's blood satisfied justice, so to forgive the confessing sinner is now the righteous thing (Wesley); the twofold blessing 'forgive... and cleanse from all unrighteousness' (pardon of guilt and purging of pollution).

The gravest denial — distinguished from v8: there the principle of sin denied ('we have no sin'), here actual sins committed ('we have not sinned,' a claim to an innocent past); 'we make him a liar' (since God throughout Scripture declares all guilty, claiming never to have sinned charges God with falsehood); Calvin: such a one blasphemes God and accuses him of untruth; 'his word is not in us' (the word that convicts of sin having no place); the climax — denying sin impugns God's truthfulness.

themes: if we say we have not sinned
his word is not in us
